citation.title: Exerting Human Control Over Decentralized Robot Swarms

extraction_metadata.notes: Re-coded task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics on 2026-07-14, bumping to v0.4/v0.10: added Known Fixed Task Object (3.5, v0.9) for the protected resource -- the article states 'the protected resource is in the middle of the open space' from the outset, previously omitted as a task-object entirely (only the moving attacker was coded, even though the Defend/Block Task behavioral-relation code already referenced 'a valuable resource'). No other v0.9/v0.10 addition applies (no object relocation, no simple known-location-arrival-only behavior beyond what Defend/Block/Pursuit/Engagement already cover, the open arena remains Land not Confined/Enclosed, and no operator-directed-computation pattern is described). study_type's 3.11 edge case doesn't apply (no Modeling/Theoretical Paper code).

study_type.justification: The paper's central contribution is two new learning-based control mechanisms (instance-based/nearest-neighbor learning for top-down global-characteristic control; evolutionary learning for bottom-up virtual-particle control), systematically validated primarily via simulation (parameter-space sampling, evolutionary fitness curves, generalization tests). A smaller confirmatory validation was also run on real Pioneer 3-AT robots (3 of 6 tested radius values), but with zero human-subject data reported anywhere — no participant ever operated the system in the paper — so Physical Robot Demonstration is Secondary rather than Primary, and no User Study code applies.

summary.abstract_summary: This paper introduces the problem of post-deployment, real-time human control over decentralized robot swarms controlled by physicomimetics (physics-inspired attractive/repulsive force laws between particle-like agents). It proposes two control mechanisms: a top-down approach that uses instance-based (nearest-neighbor) learning to map desired global swarm characteristics (e.g., swarm radius, maximum inter-agent distance) to the underlying force-law parameters that produce them, and a bottom-up approach that uses evolutionary learning to design 'virtual particles' — non-physical agents that influence real robots through the same force-law interactions — to perform higher-level swarm operations (SPLIT and FOLLOW) without altering the real agents' own parameters. Both mechanisms are demonstrated in a simulated defense scenario (a swarm of robots protecting a resource from an attacker) and validated primarily through simulation, with a smaller confirmatory test of the radius-control mechanism also run on physical Pioneer 3-AT ground robots, showing comparable accuracy (error consistently under half a meter) between simulation and hardware.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: The attacker approaches the protected resource and later retreats into a narrow corridor as it is pursued, requiring ongoing tracking rather than a one-off response.

The protected resource is a single, fixed entity positioned in the middle of the open space from the outset of the scenario, known/visible to the defender swarm throughout rather than discovered via search, matching Known Fixed Task Object (3.5, added in codebook v0.9).

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: The defender swarm's baseline role is to position around and protect a valuable resource from the adversarial attacker, thwarting its approach — the same RoboFlag-style pattern that is this code's own source example.
Once the operator splits off part of the defensive swarm to chase the attacker (SPLIT + FOLLOW), the defenders must continuously follow the already-identified, retreating attacker into a corridor.

The scenario narrative states the invader is eventually 'disabled' by the pursuing defenders, a decisive outcome directed at the already-identified attacker, though the specific disabling mechanism/action is not detailed further in the text.

team_size.justification: A single operator is described as able to request swarm-characteristic changes or trigger SPLIT/FOLLOW operations affecting the whole six-robot defender swarm.

team_composition.justification: The six defender robots (Pioneer 3-AT platforms) are identical in role and capability.
Only a single (conceptual) operator role is described; no multi-human team is present.

interaction_model.justification: A single operator's global-characteristic request or virtual-particle introduction affects the whole defender swarm simultaneously, not one robot individually.

interaction_directness.justification: The operator never addresses a command to any specific robot; instead, virtual particles introduced by the operator influence real defenders purely through their physical presence and the same local force-law interactions used among the robots themselves, and top-down characteristic requests reconfigure underlying force-law parameters rather than addressing any agent directly.

communication_proximity.justification: The operator's influence is exerted through software parameter changes / virtual-particle placement rather than physical co-location or direct physical interaction with the robots.

coordination_architecture.justification: Defender robots coordinate purely through local, pairwise physicomimetic force-law interactions with no leader robot or central coordinator described.

computational_characteristics.justification: Each robot's motion is generated by a physicomimetic force law (attractive/repulsive forces between point-mass particles based on distance), functionally equivalent to an artificial-potential-field method, the codebook's own source example for this code.

A generational evolutionary algorithm (population of 60 genomes, ranked selection, adaptive Gaussian mutation) is used to search for virtual-particle placement/parameters that achieve the SPLIT operation.

A nearest-neighbor (instance-based) learning system, trained on simulated parameter-sweep data, maps a user-requested global swarm characteristic (e.g., desired radius) to the corresponding low-level force-law parameters.

human_involvement_level.justification: The defensive swarm functions and protects the resource via autonomous physicomimetic force-law interactions without requiring operator input, while the described operator role can monitor and intervene (request characteristic changes, trigger SPLIT/FOLLOW) as needed; note this describes the system's intended design rather than a tested human interaction, since no participant actually operated the system in this paper.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: The described operator role gives discrete, mission-level directives (a desired characteristic value, or a SPLIT/FOLLOW macro-operation) that the system then autonomously executes in full via its learned parameter mappings.

operational_domain.justification: The physical validation uses Pioneer 3-AT ground robots; the simulated scenario likewise models ground-based agents.
